education.institution: Department of Management, University of Toronto - Mississauga

research.name: Multi Source Consumer Search (Job Market Paper)
research.summary: In studying consumer search behavior, models typically allow the consumer to learn the utility of a product from a single source of information. In this paper, I develop a model of sequential search where consumers who are uncertain about match values of products can receive noisy signals about them by searching a product at one of multiple sources. In the context of online learning, a consumer can learn about a product's quality typically by reading text information describing the product on a product page (Amazon) or perhaps by visiting a third-party website reviewing the product (i.e. ``cnet.com''). The consumer will consider the cost of accessing each of these sources of information (i.e. time spent searching for information, complexity of information provided) and the benefit to their learning (i.e. depth and unbiased nature of information provided). I study the consumer's optimal choices by building on the framework of Chick and Frazier (2012) to derive the consumer's optimal search rules for each decision they make (what to search, when to stop searching and purchase). I apply the model to a dataset of consumers searching for cameras online and find considerable differences in estimated search costs and learning parameters for consumers searching to learn on e-commerce websites relative to third-party review websites, suggesting that different sources of information matter for consumers' search decisions.

research.name: Aggregating Clickstream Data for Demand Prediction
research.summary: This paper develops methodology to enhance the accuracy of predictive demand models that rely on clickstream data, particularly in the context where only a limited set of product characteristics are observable. Traditional models often utilize the history of products browsed by consumers for constructing explanatory variables and measure the relationship between these past products and the target product through a distance in a characteristic space. However, this method may fall short when the available characteristics are not sufficiently informative of demand. To address this limitation, I propose a novel method for constructing latent characteristics by developing a pairwise substitution measure derived from the aggregation of product searches across all consumers. This approach leverages the entirety of consumers' search behaviors to infer substitution patterns which aides in predicting consumer purchases. I show that incorporating these latent characteristics into a demand prediction model significantly enhances its predictive accuracy, and estimate the model using a clickstream dataset from a large UK eCommerce platform.
